Appeal to authority quotes respected figures to transfer their credibility to the argument - an ethos move that strengthens claims by borrowing accepted ethos. AP English Language Unit 5 distinguishes legitimate appeal to authority (citing genuine experts in relevant fields) from fallacious appeal (citing celebrities outside their expertise, citing biased sources, citing authorities the audience does not respect). King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) cites Aquinas, Augustine, Buber, Tillich, and Niebuhr - genuine theological authorities respected by his clergy audience. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric rewards substantive use of authorities and penalizes name-dropping that fails to engage the cited author's actual reasoning.

Kairos is the classical Greek rhetorical concept of the right or opportune moment - the timeliness that makes an appeal land. AP English Language Unit 5 teaches kairos as a fourth dimension beyond Aristotle's three artistic proofs (logos, ethos, pathos). Greek rhetoricians treated kairos as crucial because the same arguments deployed at the wrong moment fall flat. JFK's Inaugural (1961, 'Let the word go forth from this time and place') seized kairos at Cold War threshold; King's 'I Have a Dream' (August 1963) chose the Lincoln Memorial centenary of the Emancipation Proclamation. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rewards kairotic analysis grounding rhetorical choices in their historical moment.

Topical kairos ties an argument to current events - the day's news, the year's crisis, the moment's debate - amplifying impact through relevance. AP English Language Unit 5 teaches topical kairos as the most common form: editorialists, op-ed writers, and speech-makers anchor arguments to what is happening now. King's 'I Have a Dream' (August 1963) tied civil rights demands to the Birmingham bombing campaign of summer 1963; Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) tied pesticide concern to postwar agricultural expansion. The AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) often presents source packets responding to topical kairos, and the 6-point rubric rewards students who place sources in their kairotic context.

Occasional kairos ties an argument to a ceremonial or seasonal occasion - inaugurations, anniversaries, holidays, dedications - leveraging the gathering's significance. AP English Language Unit 5 teaches occasional kairos through canonical texts: Lincoln's Gettysburg Address (November 1863) at the cemetery dedication, Second Inaugural (March 1865) at war's end, JFK's Inaugural (January 1961) at the presidential transition, Frederick Douglass's 1852 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' on Independence Day. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who tie rhetorical choices to occasional kairos - explaining why the choice fits the occasion's weight.

Situation appropriate kairos matches argument to context - the broader rhetorical situation including audience, exigence, constraints, and possibilities. AP English Language Unit 5 teaches situational fit as essential to all rhetoric, drawing on Lloyd Bitzer's 1968 'The Rhetorical Situation' framework. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) is rhetorically successful partly because King reads the situation precisely: a jail cell letter to clergymen has different constraints than a Mall speech to civil rights crowds. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who place rhetorical choices within their full situational context rather than analyzing texts as decontextualized artifacts.

Urgency as kairos strategy intensifies appeal by emphasizing that the moment for action is now - the window will close, the harm will compound, the opportunity will pass. AP English Language Unit 5 teaches urgency as a powerful kairotic tool but warns that manufactured urgency without grounding signals manipulation. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) builds urgency around 'the fierce urgency of now,' refuting the clergy's call for patience; Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) builds urgency around the present-tense collapse of ecosystems. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row C, sophistication) rewards essays that earn urgency through evidence rather than asserting it through pure rhetoric.

Logos, pathos, ethos are the three artistic proofs Aristotle systematized in his 'Rhetoric' (4th c. BCE) - the rhetorical triangle that forms the spine of AP English Language Unit 5. Logos is the appeal to reason through evidence, structure, and logic; pathos is the appeal to emotion through imagery, anecdote, and identification; ethos is the appeal to credibility through expertise, fairness, and good will. Aristotle insisted these three work together, not in isolation: King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) braids all three throughout. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric (Row B) rewards analysis that traces multiple appeals operating in concert rather than treating each in isolation.

Pathos, the Aristotelian appeal to emotion (Aristotle, 'Rhetoric,' Book II, 4th c. BCE), moves audiences through vivid imagery, anecdote, charged diction, and identification with shared values. AP English Language Unit 5 teaches pathos as essential but ethically loaded: pathos that aligns with logos and ethos persuades legitimately, while pure pathos manipulates. King's 'I Have a Dream' (1963) builds pathos through 'little black boys and black girls joining hands' - readers feel the future before reasoning about it. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric awards Row B points for pathos analysis tied to specific textual moves, not generic 'emotional appeal' labels.

Logos, the Aristotelian appeal to logic (Aristotle, 'Rhetoric,' Book I, 4th c. BCE), persuades through evidence, valid reasoning, and clear structure. AP English Language Unit 5 teaches logos as the appeal of inference, syllogism, statistics, and expert testimony. Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) builds logos through chemical evidence about DDT bioaccumulation; Sandel's 'Justice' (2009) builds logos through careful philosophical reasoning. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric (Row B, evidence and commentary) rewards logos analysis tied to specific reasoning structures - syllogisms, inductive chains, causal arguments - rather than generic 'use of facts' labels.

Ethos, the Aristotelian appeal to credibility (Aristotle, 'Rhetoric,' Book I, 4th c. BCE), persuades through the speaker's authority, expertise, good character, and good will toward audience. AP English Language Unit 5 teaches ethos as built through expertise, fairness, concession, appropriate qualification, and avoided fallacies (especially ad hominem). King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) builds ethos by citing Aquinas, Augustine, Buber, Tillich, and Niebuhr - demonstrating learning - and by signing it from a jail cell. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric awards ethos analysis tied to specific textual moves, distinguishing invented ethos (constructed within text) from situated ethos (carried in).

questions.prompt: Fourth appeal added in modern rhetoric.
questions.explanation: Kairos is the fourth rhetorical appeal added in modern rhetoric beyond Aristotle's three artistic proofs - the classical Greek concept of the right or opportune moment. AP English Language Unit 5 teaches kairos as essential to understanding why arguments succeed or fail historically: the same words spoken at the wrong moment fail. Lincoln's Gettysburg Address (1863) chose the kairotic moment of cemetery dedication four months after battle; King's 'I Have a Dream' (1963) chose the Emancipation centenary. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) increasingly tests kairos awareness, awarding Row C sophistication points to students who anchor analysis in kairotic context.

Alliteration is the repetition of initial consonant sounds in nearby words - 'wild and woolly,' 'pitter-patter,' 'silent Spring.' AP English Language Unit 6 classifies alliteration as a scheme (a deviation in arrangement) rather than a trope (a deviation in meaning), following classical rhetoric's distinction. Lincoln's Gettysburg Address (1863) uses subtle alliteration ('fitting and proper,' 'last full measure'); JFK's Inaugural (1961) deploys 'pay any price, bear any burden, meet any hardship.' Alliteration aids memorability and creates sonic emphasis. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who identify alliteration and tie it to specific effects (emphasis, mnemonic force, tonal weight) rather than noting it descriptively.

Anaphora is the repetition of the same word or phrase at the beginning of successive clauses, sentences, or paragraphs - one of the most powerful schemes in oral rhetoric. AP English Language Unit 6 makes anaphora central because it appears constantly in canonical texts: King's 'I Have a Dream' (1963) builds 'I have a dream' and 'Let freedom ring' as anaphoric pillars; Lincoln's Gettysburg Address (1863) closes with 'of the people, by the people, for the people'; Frederick Douglass's 1852 speech uses anaphora ('I will use the severest language I can command'). The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards anaphora analysis tied to emphasis, rhythm, and structural framing of the argument.

Epistrophe is the repetition of the same word or phrase at the end of successive clauses - the mirror image of anaphora, less common but rhetorically powerful for driving home a conclusion. AP English Language Unit 6 teaches epistrophe through Lincoln's Gettysburg Address (1863): 'government of the people, by the people, for the people' - the repetition of 'the people' as terminal phrase. Epistrophe leverages the recency effect: the repeated word stays in the ear after the clause ends. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who distinguish epistrophe from anaphora and analyze its specific effect - typically driving home the concept the repetition names rather than the action the clauses describe.

Chiasmus is a scheme of inverted word order - a phrase or sentence whose second half mirrors the first in reverse, producing memorable balanced structure. AP English Language Unit 6 teaches chiasmus through canonical examples: JFK's Inaugural (1961) 'Ask not what your country can do for you - ask what you can do for your country' reverses 'country/you' to 'you/country'; the same speech's 'Let us never negotiate out of fear, but let us never fear to negotiate' inverts negotiate/fear. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rewards chiasmus analysis tied to effect: the inversion typically produces aphoristic punch, memorable rhythm, and forced re-evaluation of the relationship between paired terms.

Parallelism is the use of similar grammatical structure for similar ideas - matching nouns to nouns, verbs to verbs, clauses to clauses - producing balance and clarity. AP English Language Unit 6 teaches parallelism as the scaffolding scheme underlying anaphora, epistrophe, and chiasmus: all repetition-based schemes depend on parallel structure. Lincoln's Gettysburg Address (1863) builds parallelism: 'we cannot dedicate - we cannot consecrate - we cannot hallow'; King's 'I Have a Dream' (1963) sustains parallel construction throughout. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards parallelism analysis specific enough to identify the structures matched and the rhetorical effect of matching them - typically emphasis, clarity, or rhythmic momentum.

Metaphor is a comparison that asserts identity between two unlike things without using 'like' or 'as' - the most powerful trope because it maps the structure of one domain onto another. AP English Language Unit 6 teaches metaphor as foundational figurative language (Aristotle in 'Poetics,' 4th c. BCE, called metaphor the greatest mark of genius). King's 'I Have a Dream' (1963) builds extended metaphor of America's 'promissory note' to Black citizens; Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) builds metaphor of the silenced soundscape. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards specific metaphor analysis tied to the source/target mapping and its rhetorical effect, not generic 'figurative language' labels.

Simile is an explicit comparison using 'like' or 'as' - similar to metaphor but more cautious, marking the comparison as analogy rather than asserting identity. AP English Language Unit 6 distinguishes simile from metaphor: 'America is a melting pot' (metaphor) vs 'America is like a melting pot' (simile). Similes preserve the comparative distance while still mapping structures across domains. Memoir and personal essay writers often prefer similes for their explicit signaling: Amy Tan's 'Mother Tongue' (1990) deploys similes in describing her mother's English. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rewards students who analyze similes specifically rather than treating them as weaker metaphors - simile's explicitness can be its rhetorical strength.

Personification is the attribution of human qualities to non-human entities - animals, objects, abstractions, places - making the inanimate feel agentive and the abstract feel particular. AP English Language Unit 6 teaches personification as a primary trope for pathos: readers respond to personified entities as they would to characters. Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) personifies nature throughout, building pathos for ecological loss; Wollstonecraft's 'Vindication' (1792) personifies reason, virtue, and tyranny. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards specific personification identification tied to its rhetorical work - typically making abstractions feel like agents readers can identify with or oppose.

Hyperbole is deliberate exaggeration for rhetorical effect - overstating beyond literal truth to convey intensity, urgency, or emotion. AP English Language Unit 6 teaches hyperbole as a trope readers must recognize as intentional rather than literal: the writer is not lying but signaling emotional truth through measured overstatement. Frederick Douglass's 1852 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' deploys hyperbolic language about American hypocrisy that exceeds literal claim while accurately naming moral intensity. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who identify hyperbole and analyze its function - typically intensifying pathos while maintaining ethos through the audience's recognition of intentional exaggeration.

Litotes is a form of understatement that affirms by negating the contrary - 'not bad' meaning 'good,' 'no small matter' meaning 'significant matter.' AP English Language Unit 6 teaches litotes as a sophisticated trope creating ironic distance, dry wit, or rhetorical restraint. Litotes appears throughout English literary tradition from Anglo-Saxon poetry through modern essay. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who recognize litotes (often missed because it can read as simple understatement) and analyze its rhetorical effect: typically producing ironic emphasis, conveying confidence through restraint, or maintaining decorum while making strong claims. Litotes pairs with formal voice in academic and ceremonial prose.

Verbal irony is saying the opposite of what one means, with the gap between surface and intended meaning available to the audience. AP English Language Unit 6 distinguishes verbal irony from sarcasm (verbal irony with hostile intent) and from situational and dramatic irony (which depend on circumstance, not speech). Swift's 'A Modest Proposal' (1729) sustains verbal irony throughout: the calm proposal to eat Irish infants is the surface; the savage indignation against English exploitation is the actual meaning. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) routinely tests verbal irony, and the 6-point rubric penalizes students who read ironic texts literally - a documented pitfall where surface meaning is taken as author position.

Situational irony is a discrepancy between expected and actual outcome - what happens contradicts what should have happened given context. AP English Language Unit 6 teaches situational irony as a rhetorical feature of narrative and essay rather than figurative language proper: Sojourner Truth's 1851 speech leverages situational irony in the gap between Christian women's claims of weakness and her actual physical labor as enslaved woman. Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) builds situational irony around chemicals introduced to control pests destroying the broader ecosystem. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rewards students who identify situational irony and analyze its argumentative work in exposing assumptions.

questions.prompt: Reader knows what character does not.
questions.explanation: Dramatic irony is the discrepancy between what a character knows and what the audience knows - the audience holds information that gives the character's words or actions meaning beyond the character's awareness. AP English Language Unit 6 places dramatic irony primarily in literary contexts but notes its presence in essay when authors construct personas who do not know what readers know. Swift's 'A Modest Proposal' (1729) generates dramatic irony as the calm proposer remains oblivious to the moral horror readers feel. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rewards students who notice dramatic irony's rhetorical function - typically generating critique through the persona's blindness to what the audience sees.

questions.prompt: Use of mocking to expose folly.
questions.explanation: Sarcasm is verbal irony deployed with hostile or mocking intent - saying the opposite of what one means in order to wound, ridicule, or expose. AP English Language Unit 6 distinguishes sarcasm from broader verbal irony: all sarcasm is verbal irony, but not all verbal irony is sarcasm. Frederick Douglass's 1852 speech deploys sarcasm against American slaveholders' Independence Day celebrations: 'This Fourth of July is yours, not mine.' Swift's 'Modest Proposal' (1729) lifts beyond sarcasm into sustained satirical fiction. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who distinguish sarcasm from non-hostile irony and analyze how sarcasm functions argumentatively - typically as rebuke or expose.

Satire is a literary form using ridicule, irony, and exaggeration to critique vices and follies - a genre rather than a single rhetorical device. AP English Language Unit 6 teaches satire through Swift's 'A Modest Proposal' (1729), the canonical example, and through Orwell's essays. Satire requires readers to detect the gap between surface and intended meaning and to direct critique at the satirized target rather than the satirical persona. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) sometimes uses satirical passages, and the 6-point rubric heavily penalizes students who read satire literally - a documented failure where surface advocacy is taken as author position. Strong satire analysis traces the critique through specific ironic moves.

A rhetorical question is a question asked for effect rather than answer - to provoke reflection, emphasize a point, or shape audience response without requiring response. AP English Language Unit 6 teaches rhetorical questions as a primary tool of oral rhetoric and persuasive essay. Sojourner Truth's 1851 speech repeats 'And ain't I a woman?' as rhetorical question demanding the audience confront the gap between her treatment and the speaker's claims about women; Frederick Douglass's 1852 speech asks 'What to the slave is the Fourth of July?' The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rewards students who analyze rhetorical questions specifically rather than treating them as actual queries - the rhetorical work happens through the implied answer.

An oxymoron is the deliberate juxtaposition of two contradictory terms in a single phrase - 'jumbo shrimp,' 'deafening silence,' 'living death.' AP English Language Unit 6 teaches oxymoron as a compressed paradox: the apparent contradiction signals a deeper truth resistant to flat statement. Milton's 'darkness visible' from Paradise Lost is canonical; modern essay deploys oxymoron sparingly for emphasis. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rewards students who identify oxymoron and analyze its specific effect - typically forcing readers to hold contradictory ideas together to discover the writer's nuanced claim, creating rhetorical density that resists summary.

A paradox is an apparent contradiction that, on examination, reveals a deeper truth - a statement that seems self-defeating but illuminates rather than collapses. AP English Language Unit 6 teaches paradox as related to but larger than oxymoron: paradoxes are full statements while oxymorons are compressed phrases. Orwell's 'Politics and the English Language' (1946) traffics in paradox about how clear thought requires clear language and clear language requires clear thought. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who identify paradox and trace how it functions - typically forcing readers through apparent contradiction toward more sophisticated understanding, building ethos through demonstrated intellectual depth.

A euphemism is a mild or indirect word substituted for a harsh or unpleasant one - 'passed away' for died, 'collateral damage' for civilian deaths, 'enhanced interrogation' for torture. AP English Language Unit 6 teaches euphemism as ethically loaded: euphemism can be merciful (softening grief) or evasive (concealing reality). Orwell's 'Politics and the English Language' (1946) directly attacks political euphemism as a tool for making lies sound truthful: 'the language of pacification' for bombing villages. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who identify euphemism and analyze its function critically, distinguishing legitimate decorum from evasive political language.

questions.prompt: Calling on absent or imaginary thing.
questions.explanation: Apostrophe is the rhetorical figure of addressing an absent person, abstraction, or imaginary thing as if it were present and able to respond - 'O Death, where is thy sting?' 'O Liberty!' AP English Language Unit 6 teaches apostrophe as a high-register classical figure (distinguished from the punctuation mark) creating emotional intensity through direct address. Frederick Douglass's 1852 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' apostrophizes the absent founding fathers; ceremonial speeches address abstractions like Liberty, Justice, History. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who identify apostrophe and analyze its rhetorical effect - typically elevating tone, intensifying pathos, and creating presence for what is absent.

Assonance is the repetition of vowel sounds in nearby words - 'the rain in Spain stays mainly in the plain,' 'fleet feet sweep by sleeping geese.' AP English Language Unit 6 teaches assonance as a sonic device creating mood, emphasis, and memorability without the harder edges of consonant repetition. Assonance is more common in poetry than essay but appears in rhetorically polished prose. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who identify assonance in passages where sound shapes meaning, analyzing how the vowel music supports tonal effect - typically smoothness, length, or contemplative mood. Assonance pairs with parallelism and other schemes to build oratorical polish.

Consonance is the repetition of consonant sounds in nearby words - the harder counterpart to assonance, often crisper and more emphatic. AP English Language Unit 6 distinguishes consonance from alliteration: alliteration requires initial position, while consonance can occur anywhere in the word ('pitter-patter,' 'pitter' and 'patter' share the t and tt consonance, plus initial alliteration). Lincoln's prose uses consonance subtly to build rhythmic weight. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who notice consonance and trace its effect - typically punchy emphasis, sonic tightness, or atmospheric mood depending on the specific consonants and their density.

Onomatopoeia is the use of words that imitate the sounds they describe - 'buzz,' 'hiss,' 'crash,' 'whisper,' 'pop.' AP English Language Unit 6 teaches onomatopoeia as a sonic device making language physically reproduce the referent, intensifying imagery's vividness. Onomatopoeia is more common in narrative and poetry than analytical essay but appears in descriptive passages. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who identify onomatopoeia and analyze its rhetorical effect - typically grounding abstract claims in sensory immediacy, building pathos through embodied response. Nature writers and memoirists deploy onomatopoeia to draw readers into scenes through sound.

End rhyme is the repetition of identical or similar sounds at the ends of lines in poetry or in carefully composed prose. AP English Language Unit 6 includes end rhyme primarily for student awareness in literary contexts; analytical essay rarely deploys it. When end rhyme appears in prose, it typically signals ceremonial register or rhetorical heightening: speeches occasionally close on rhymed couplets for memorability. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rarely tests end rhyme specifically, but students who notice it in passages can analyze its effect - typically signaling oral tradition, poetic register, or rhetorical heightening that the writer wants the audience to remember.

Internal rhyme is the repetition of identical or similar sounds within a line of poetry or sentence of prose - 'the splendor falls on castle walls' (Tennyson) or 'while I nodded, nearly napping' (Poe). AP English Language Unit 6 teaches internal rhyme as a sonic device creating musical effect within rather than between rhetorical units. Internal rhyme appears occasionally in heightened prose, especially in passages designed for oral delivery or memorable phrasing. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rewards students who notice internal rhyme in passages and analyze its function - typically tightening rhythm, building sonic emphasis, or signaling the writer's heightened rhetorical purpose at the moment of its appearance.

A thesis is the main claim of an essay - the controlling argument the rest of the text exists to defend. AP English Language Unit 7 frames the thesis as the make-or-break feature of any argument essay: without a defensible thesis, no amount of evidence or style can earn full credit. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row A, 1 point, College Board CED 2024) requires a thesis that takes a defensible position responsive to the prompt; pure restatement, vague claims, or absolutist positions cap the score. Strong AP theses are specific, defensible, responsive, and complex enough to organize the body paragraphs that follow. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) sets out its thesis early about the moral duty to break unjust laws.

Support is the evidence backing the claim - the data, examples, expert testimony, anecdotes, and statistics that give the argument empirical weight. AP English Language Unit 7 frames support as one of the three Toulmin components (claim, data, warrant) introduced in 'The Uses of Argument' (1958). The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row B, up to 4 points evidence and commentary) requires specific, relevant support paired with commentary that explains its force; evidence dumped without commentary loses Row B points. Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) supports its policy thesis with documented bird die-offs, chemical residue studies, and dose-response data - the evidence/commentary integration AP rubrics reward.

A warrant is the reasoning principle connecting evidence to claim - the often-unstated assumption that licenses the inference. AP English Language Unit 7 makes warrant analysis central because most argument failures occur not at evidence but at warrant: writers assume connecting principles audiences do not share. Toulmin's 'The Uses of Argument' (1958) introduced warrant as the field-dependent reasoning principle that turns data into support. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row B, evidence and commentary) explicitly rewards commentary that surfaces and defends warrants - this is what distinguishes commentary from mere repetition. Strong AP essays make warrants explicit when audiences might resist them.

Counterargument is the opposing view acknowledged within one's own argument - either accepted as a limit or refuted in turn. AP English Language Unit 7 treats counterargument as essential to mature argument because ignoring opposition signals either ignorance or evasion, both fatal to ethos. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row C, sophistication, 1 point, College Board CED 2024) explicitly rewards 'engaging effectively with broader context' and 'addressing complexities or tensions' - language that demands counterargument engagement. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) systematically engages each clergy charge before refuting; essays that fail to engage obvious objections cap at 4 of 6 regardless of style.

Refutation is the move of disproving or undermining the counterargument - the active critique that completes the rhetorical work of acknowledging opposition. AP English Language Unit 7 teaches refutation as following concession in the Toulmin scheme: the writer concedes what is true in the opposing view, then identifies its decisive failure. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) refutes the charge of extremism by reframing himself in the company of Jesus, Paul, Luther, Lincoln, and Jefferson - inverting the term's valence. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row B, evidence and commentary) rewards refutation that engages the strongest version of opposition (steel-man) rather than caricature (straw man).

An argument of fact asserts that something is or is not the case - a verifiable empirical proposition demanding evidence rather than evaluation. AP English Language Unit 7 teaches arguments of fact as the first of three claim types (fact, value, policy) from Toulmin's 'The Uses of Argument' (1958). Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) makes factual arguments about DDT bioaccumulation, supported by chemical evidence; modern climate science arguments are arguments of fact about temperature trends and CO2 levels. The AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) often requires distinguishing factual claims from evaluative ones in source packets, and the 6-point rubric rewards students who cite appropriate evidence for each claim type.

An argument of value asserts that something is good or bad, just or unjust, beautiful or ugly - a normative claim requiring criteria-based evaluation rather than purely empirical data. AP English Language Unit 7 treats arguments of value as a distinct category (Toulmin, 1958): the evidence must include both empirical features and the criteria by which they count as good or bad. Sojourner Truth's 1851 speech makes the value claim of human equality; King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) makes value claims about just versus unjust laws, citing Aquinas and Augustine. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric rewards essays that recognize criteria explicitly rather than asserting value claims as if they were factual.

An argument of policy calls for action or change - a prescriptive claim asserting that something should or should not be done. AP English Language Unit 7 frames policy arguments as the most demanding because they require establishing fact (the problem exists), value (it matters morally), and feasibility (the proposed remedy is workable). Toulmin's 'The Uses of Argument' (1958) identifies policy as the third major claim type. Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) culminates in policy claims about pesticide regulation; JFK's Inaugural (1961, 'Ask not what your country can do for you') issued a policy claim about civic obligation. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) frequently prompts policy claims, and the 6-point rubric rewards feasibility analysis, not just desirability.

Rogerian argument is an approach (Carl Rogers, 1951; later codified by Young, Becker, and Pike) that seeks common ground rather than victory - establishing shared interests and acknowledging the opposing view's strengths before introducing the writer's position. AP English Language Unit 7 contrasts Rogerian argument with Aristotelian (adversarial) and Toulminian (analytical) approaches: Rogerian is well-suited to deeply polarized debates where confrontation hardens opposition. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) blends Aristotelian and Rogerian features. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row C, sophistication) rewards Rogerian moves when prompts touch contentious topics.

Aristotelian argument is the classical adversarial approach codified in Aristotle's 'Rhetoric' (4th c. BCE) - the writer takes a clear position and defends it through logos, ethos, and pathos, refuting opposition. AP English Language Unit 7 teaches Aristotelian argument as the default structure of most Western argumentation, distinct from Rogerian (consensus-seeking) and Toulminian (analytical). The five-part classical arrangement (exordium, narration, partition, argument, peroration) from Cicero's 'De Inventione' (c. 84 BCE) and Quintilian's 'Institutio Oratoria' (c. 95 CE) refined the Aristotelian frame. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) typically rewards Aristotelian structure: clear thesis, evidence-driven support, counterargument acknowledged and refuted.

Logos is persuasion via logical appeal - the Aristotelian artistic proof grounded in evidence, valid reasoning, and clear inference, central to AP English Language Unit 7 argumentation. Logos persuades through statistics, syllogism, expert testimony, empirical evidence, and reasoning chains. Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) builds logos through chemical evidence; Sandel's 'Justice' (2009) builds logos through philosophical reasoning. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row B, up to 4 points) rewards logos-driven evidence and commentary specifically, distinguishing reasoning from mere assertion. Pure logos without ethos or pathos rarely persuades real audiences; Aristotle's 'Rhetoric' (4th c. BCE) emphasized that the three appeals work together.

Pathos is persuasion via emotional appeal - the Aristotelian artistic proof grounded in imagery, anecdote, charged diction, and identification with shared values. AP English Language Unit 7 teaches pathos as essential to argument because pure logos rarely moves audiences to action: the analytical proof must be felt to land. King's 'I Have a Dream' (1963) deploys pathos through imagery of 'little black boys and black girls joining hands'; Sojourner Truth's 1851 speech mobilizes pathos through counted lost children. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row C, sophistication, 1 point) rewards essays where pathos serves the argument rather than substitutes for it - earned pathos integrated with logos and ethos.

Ethos is persuasion via credibility - the Aristotelian artistic proof grounded in the speaker's expertise, fairness, good character, and good will toward audience. AP English Language Unit 7 frames ethos as built through demonstrated knowledge (expertise), strategic concession (fairness), avoided fallacies (especially ad hominem), and appropriate qualification (intellectual honesty). King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) builds ethos through citation of Aquinas, Augustine, Buber, Tillich, and Niebuhr. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric awards Row C sophistication partly on the basis of ethos: essays that maintain reasonable, fair-minded persona while making strong claims earn more than those that demonize opposition.

Kairos is persuasion via right timing - the classical Greek concept of the opportune moment, added to Aristotle's three artistic proofs as a fourth dimension in modern rhetoric. AP English Language Unit 7 teaches kairos as essential to argumentative success: the same case made at the wrong moment fails. King's 'I Have a Dream' (1963) chose the Lincoln Memorial centenary of the Emancipation Proclamation; JFK's Inaugural (1961) seized the Cold War threshold. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row C, sophistication) rewards essays that situate their claims kairotically - engaging the cultural moment rather than treating the prompt's question as abstract or timeless.

Identification (Kenneth Burke, 'A Rhetoric of Motives,' 1950) is the strategy of building common ground with the audience - establishing shared identity, values, or interests that allow persuasion to proceed. AP English Language Unit 7 teaches identification as Burke's modern reframing of classical ethos and pathos: persuasion requires the audience to feel the speaker is 'one of us.' King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) identifies with the clergymen as fellow Christians, citing shared scripture; Frederick Douglass's 1852 speech invites white audiences to identify with the moral position the day's hypocrisy denies. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rewards identification moves that build rather than fracture audience common ground.

A rhetorical choice is any deliberate stylistic or structural decision a writer makes to achieve a purpose with a specific audience - the unit of analysis on the AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2). AP English Language Unit 7 frames rhetorical choices as everything a writer could have done differently: every diction selection, syntactic arrangement, organizational pattern, and tonal register is a choice with effects. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric (Row B, evidence and commentary, up to 4 points) explicitly rewards analysis of rhetorical choices - the College Board CED 2024 language - rather than mere identification of devices. Strong commentary names the choice, analyzes its effect, and ties it to author purpose.

Diction as rhetorical choice is the writer's selection of vocabulary - word choice analyzed in terms of formal/informal, abstract/concrete, denotative/connotative, Anglo-Saxon/Latinate. AP English Language Unit 7 treats diction as the most basic stylistic choice because every word selection shapes meaning. Orwell's 'Politics and the English Language' (1946) makes diction the central concern: pretentious diction conceals political reality. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) shifts diction strategically - high formal when citing Aquinas, plain Anglo-Saxon when describing lived oppression. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards specific diction analysis tied to author purpose, not generic 'word choice' labels.

Syntax as rhetorical choice is the writer's arrangement of words in sentences - sentence type, length, complexity, word order, and pattern. AP English Language Unit 7 teaches syntactic analysis as parallel to diction analysis: every sentence could have been shaped differently, and the shaping creates effects. JFK's Inaugural (1961) uses chiasmus, periodic sentences, and parallel structure as deliberate syntactic choices; Lincoln's Gettysburg Address (1863) deploys parallelism and elevated periodic constructions. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric (Row B) rewards syntactic analysis tied to specific effects (emphasis, suspense, balance, rhythm) rather than naming sentence types abstractly.

Tone as rhetorical choice is the author's attitude toward subject matter - the emotional and intellectual register shaped through diction, syntax, imagery, and other stylistic features. AP English Language Unit 7 treats tone as a primary unit of rhetorical analysis because the same content delivered in different tones has different effects: indignant vs. measured, satirical vs. solemn, conciliatory vs. confrontational. Swift's 'A Modest Proposal' (1729) sustains deadpan tone to intensify satirical critique; King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) sustains firm but conciliatory tone. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards tonal analysis with precise adjectives tied to specific textual evidence.

Organization as rhetorical choice is the writer's structural pattern - chronological, cause-effect, problem-solution, compare-contrast, climactic, or topical. AP English Language Unit 7 teaches organizational choice as one of the higher-order rhetorical decisions, encompassing the third canon of classical rhetoric (dispositio in Cicero's 'De Inventione,' c. 84 BCE). Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) follows problem-solution organization; King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) follows the clergy's points in order, then synthesizes. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards organization analysis when students explain why the pattern fits the author's purpose - not just identifying the pattern.

Audience analysis is the systematic study of the audience's knowledge, values, disposition, and likely responses - foundational to all rhetorical choice. AP English Language Unit 7 teaches audience analysis as the prerequisite to all other rhetorical decisions: writers who do not know their audience cannot calibrate logos, ethos, or pathos. Edwin Black's 'Second Persona' (1970) extended audience analysis to imagined audiences constructed by the text. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) is a master class in layered audience analysis: clergy, white moderates, Black community, posterity. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who tie rhetorical choices to specific audience features rather than treating rhetoric as universal.

Adapting to audience is the practice of calibrating rhetorical choices to audience features - selecting evidence the audience finds credible, deploying values the audience holds, anticipating objections the audience will raise. AP English Language Unit 7 treats adaptation as essential because the same case made the same way fails different audiences. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) adapts by citing Christian theologians for clergy audience and grounding civil disobedience in their own tradition. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row C, sophistication) rewards essays that recognize audience implicitly through evidence selection, framing, and tone, even though prompts rarely name an explicit audience.

A hostile audience opposes the speaker's position and requires concession, common ground, and careful ethos-building before substantive argument can land. AP English Language Unit 7 teaches that hostile audiences trigger maximum rhetorical work: writers must overcome resistance before introducing claims. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) addresses a hostile audience of white clergy who called him an outside agitator; he opens by establishing his organizational ties and citing scripture they share. Wollstonecraft's 'Vindication' (1792) addresses the hostile audience of Enlightenment men. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ rewards students who identify hostile-audience strategies: heavy concession, layered ethos-building, careful refutation.

A friendly audience shares the speaker's position and requires reinforcement, energy, and a renewed call to action rather than persuasion to a new view. AP English Language Unit 7 distinguishes friendly audiences from hostile and neutral ones because the rhetorical work differs: friendly audiences need consolidation rather than conversion. King's 'I Have a Dream' (1963) addressed a largely friendly civil rights audience on the National Mall - the rhetoric celebrates and energizes; JFK's Inaugural (1961) addressed a friendly American electorate at the moment of his ascension. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ rewards students who recognize friendly-audience features and analyze how rhetoric works to mobilize rather than convert.

A neutral audience holds no strong view on the issue, often because they have not engaged with it - the most easily persuaded because they have not formed commitments resisting change. AP English Language Unit 7 distinguishes neutral audiences from undecided ones (who actively weigh evidence) and from hostile ones (who resist actively). Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) targeted a largely neutral suburban audience whose primary concern was lawn care; her 'fable for tomorrow' opening engaged them through narrative before introducing argument. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ rewards students who identify neutral-audience strategies: engagement first, complexity introduced gradually, evidence dense enough to overcome inertia.

questions.prompt: Bringing sources together to support thesis.
questions.explanation: Synthesis is the integration of multiple sources to support an original thesis - the core skill tested by FRQ 1 (synthesis essay) on the AP English Language exam. AP English Language Unit 8 distinguishes synthesis from summary (restating what sources say) and from research-paper compilation (citing many sources sequentially): synthesis requires the student writer to use sources as evidence and conversation partners for an argument the student owns. The AP synthesis FRQ (Q1, 6-point rubric, College Board CED 2024) awards Row B evidence/commentary points (up to 4) for synthesis that integrates sources via the writer's reasoning, not for sources cited without commentary or for commentary unsupported by sources.

questions.prompt: Number of sources required in FRQ.
questions.explanation: Three sources is the minimum number students must incorporate in the AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) to earn evidence points - specifically, the Row B rubric requires at least three of the provided sources used in support of the student's thesis. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches that students should engage more sources when possible (the packet typically contains six or seven), but three is the floor for evidence credit. The College Board CED 2024 explicitly directs students to 'incorporate at least three of the provided sources' as the procedural baseline. Strong essays integrate sources rather than parading them, treating each as evidence for the controlling argument.

Multiple media in the AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) source packet refers to the variety of source types students encounter - editorials, charts, photographs, op-eds, primary sources, scholarly excerpts, data visualizations. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches that visual sources (charts, images, cartoons) require the same rhetorical analysis as textual ones: identifying purpose, audience, evidence, and bias. The AP synthesis FRQ rubric (Row B) rewards students who integrate visual and textual sources alongside each other rather than skipping visual sources or treating them superficially. Pollan, Carson, and other modern essayists routinely braid statistics, charts, and prose to mirror the multi-media synthesis FRQ challenge.

In-text citation in the AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) means identifying which source a student is using within the essay's prose - typically by Source A, Source B, or by author name in parentheses. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches that the synthesis FRQ does not require full MLA formal citation but does require attribution clear enough for the reader to identify which source the student is citing. The AP synthesis rubric (Row B) penalizes evidence used without attribution - the reader cannot credit unattributed claims as drawn from sources. Strong essays integrate citation smoothly: 'As Source A notes...' or '(Source A)' parenthetically.

A synthesis thesis is an original argument using sources rather than merely summarizing them - the controlling claim of the AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) response. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches that the synthesis thesis must take a defensible position responsive to the prompt and use the source packet as evidence and conversation. The AP synthesis FRQ rubric (Row A, 1 point, College Board CED 2024) requires a defensible thesis; pure summary of sources or restatement of the prompt earns zero. Strong synthesis theses are specific (not 'this is complex'), defensible (not absolutist), and clearly the student's own argument supported by but not reducible to the sources.

Paraphrase is restating a source's content in the student writer's own words and sentence structures - the standard form of source integration in academic writing. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches paraphrase as central to synthesis because it lets the writer subordinate the source to her own argument's flow, rather than letting quoted passages disrupt the prose. Effective paraphrase preserves the source's meaning while changing the language; weak paraphrase shifts a few words and risks plagiarism. The AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) rewards paraphrased integration when followed by analytical commentary, distinguishing paraphrase + commentary from mere paraphrase. The College Board CED 2024 expects students to combine paraphrase, summary, and quotation strategically.

Quotation is direct copying of source language inside quotation marks, with attribution - reserved for moments where the source's exact wording matters (memorable phrasing, technical precision, signature voice). AP English Language Unit 8 teaches selective quotation as a sign of sophisticated source use: over-quotation suggests the writer cannot translate sources into her own argument. The AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) rubric (Row B) rewards quotation when integrated with commentary explaining why the quoted language matters and how it supports the argument. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) demonstrates skilled quotation: he quotes Aquinas, Augustine, and Tillich strategically rather than throughout.

questions.prompt: Brief restatement of source's main idea.
questions.explanation: Summary is brief restatement of a source's main idea - typically used for context, background, or quick reference. AP English Language Unit 8 distinguishes summary (whole or substantial part) from paraphrase (specific passage) and from quotation (verbatim). Summary is appropriate when students need to orient readers to a source without deep engagement. The AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) rubric does not penalize summary directly but caps evidence credit when summary substitutes for analysis: Row B requires commentary connecting source content to argument. Strong essays use summary efficiently, then add the analytical commentary the rubric rewards.

A signal phrase is the introductory language leading into a quotation or paraphrase - 'As Source A argues,' 'Pollan notes,' 'According to Carson' - integrating attribution smoothly into the prose. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches signal phrases as the technical move that distinguishes integrated source use from dropped quotation. Strong signal phrases name the source, often suggest its character (argues vs. concedes vs. reports), and connect the source's content to the writer's argument. The AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) rewards signal phrases that integrate evidence into argument rather than orphan quotations that interrupt prose flow without analytical setup.

Plagiarism is the use of another's words or ideas without proper credit - the most serious academic offense, leading to score-zero penalties on AP essays and academic discipline beyond. AP English Language Unit 8 treats plagiarism prevention as foundational to source use: students must attribute everything not their own, including paraphrases and ideas as well as quotations. The College Board CED 2024 emphasizes academic integrity in synthesis writing. On the AP synthesis FRQ (Q1), unattributed source material costs evidence credit (the reader cannot identify which source supplies what) and risks ethical violations. Strong essays attribute generously - over-attribution is far safer than under-attribution.

A thesis statement in synthesis writing is the controlling claim - the original argument the synthesis essay defends using source evidence. AP English Language Unit 8 frames the synthesis thesis as different from research-paper claims: rather than reviewing what experts have said, the student must take her own position responsive to the prompt and use sources to support that position. The AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) rubric (Row A, 1 point, College Board CED 2024) requires a defensible thesis; weak thesis types lose the point: pure summary of sources, restatement of the prompt, vague claims ('this is complex'), or unsupportable absolutes. Strong synthesis theses are specific and defensible.

Evidence in synthesis writing is the source material - quotations, paraphrases, summaries, statistics, anecdotes drawn from the AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) source packet - that supports the thesis. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches evidence selection: students should choose sources that genuinely support specific claims, not just include sources to hit the three-source minimum. The AP synthesis FRQ rubric (Row B, up to 4 points evidence and commentary) awards points only when evidence is integrated with commentary explaining its support function. Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) selects evidence rigorously - each piece of chemical evidence serves a specific argumentative move.

Analysis in synthesis writing is the reasoning linking sources to thesis - the commentary that explains why the cited evidence supports the controlling claim. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches analysis as the Row B make-or-break for the synthesis FRQ (Q1): evidence dumped without commentary loses Row B points because the reader cannot follow the logical link from source to claim. Strong analysis surfaces warrants (Toulmin, 1958), engages source bias, considers alternative interpretations, and ties each source to the thesis explicitly. The College Board CED 2024 emphasizes 'commentary that explains the relationship between evidence and claim' as the rubric standard.

Counterargument in synthesis writing is the acknowledgment of opposing sources or perspectives within the synthesis FRQ (Q1) packet - using the source diversity to build sophistication. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches counterargument as essential to mature synthesis: most synthesis packets include sources on multiple sides of the issue, and strong essays engage opposing sources rather than cherry-picking supportive ones. The AP synthesis FRQ rubric (Row C, sophistication, 1 point) rewards essays that engage source disagreement rather than ignoring it. Pure source-stacking on one side, when the packet offers diverse perspectives, signals shallow synthesis.

A conclusion in synthesis writing is the final synthesis claim - the take-away that consolidates the argument and gives the reader closure. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches that effective synthesis conclusions can extend the argument's implications, return to a framing source, or articulate broader stakes. The AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) rubric (Row A) accepts a thesis stated in introduction or conclusion, so the conclusion is a legitimate place to land the thesis if rhetorically warranted. The Row C sophistication point often turns on whether the conclusion earns its weight through earned insight or merely restates the opening.

Source selection is the strategic choice of which sources from the AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) packet to use in the essay - typically three to five of the six or seven provided. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches that the choice matters: not all sources serve the student's argument equally well, and weaker essays often cite all sources cursorily while stronger essays develop fewer sources thoroughly. The AP synthesis FRQ rubric (Row B, evidence and commentary) rewards depth of integration over breadth of citation, so students should select sources they can engage analytically rather than parading the full packet. The College Board CED 2024 emphasizes 'judicious use' of the source packet.

Source grouping is the strategy of categorizing sources by viewpoint, theme, or rhetorical function before drafting - mapping the AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) packet to find natural argumentative structures. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches source grouping as a prewriting technique: students might group sources by position (pro/con/qualified), by type (data/anecdote/expert), or by argumentative function (establishing problem, proposing solution, anticipating objection). The grouping reveals the synthesis architecture: which sources team up on each major claim, which oppose, which qualify. The AP synthesis FRQ rubric rewards essays whose organization emerges from genuine source relationships rather than source-by-source plodding.

Cross-source analysis is the practice of comparing two or more sources directly within the synthesis essay - putting them in conversation rather than treating each in isolation. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches cross-source analysis as a sophistication marker: the AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) rubric (Row C, 1 point) rewards essays that engage source relationships, especially when sources disagree or qualify each other. Cross-source analysis demonstrates that the writer has read the packet as a whole, not just mined isolated quotes. Strong AP synthesis essays often include paragraphs that explicitly juxtapose sources: 'While Source A claims X, Source D's data complicates the picture by showing Y.'

Critical reading is the practice of acknowledging source bias, evaluating evidence, and questioning assumptions within the AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) source packet. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches critical reading as essential to credible synthesis: students who treat all sources as equally authoritative miss the rhetorical work of source evaluation. The AP synthesis FRQ rubric (Row B, evidence and commentary) rewards critical engagement with source bias - acknowledging that an editorial pushes a position, that a corporate-sponsored study has interests, that a primary source comes from a particular vantage. Critical reading builds student ethos by demonstrating she can be trusted with the evidence.

Original synthesis is the central goal of the AP synthesis FRQ (Q1): building an argument from the sources that is the student's own, not reducible to any single source's position. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches that original synthesis requires the student to take a stance the source packet supports but does not state - using sources as evidence and conversation partners rather than authorities to defer to. The AP synthesis FRQ rubric (Row A, 1 point, College Board CED 2024) requires a defensible thesis; Row C (1 point) rewards sophistication including 'engaging effectively with broader context.' Original synthesis is what distinguishes 5- and 6-point essays from mere source compilation.

Citation is the identification of source within the synthesis essay - the procedural marker telling the reader which source supports which claim. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches that the synthesis FRQ (Q1) does not require formal academic citation (MLA Works Cited, footnotes) but does require attribution clear enough for the reader to identify the cited source. The AP synthesis FRQ rubric (Row B, evidence and commentary) implicitly requires citation: unattributed evidence cannot be credited because the reader cannot verify it comes from the packet. Strong essays cite by source letter (Source A, Source C) or by author name parenthetically.

MLA (Modern Language Association) is the citation style standard for English studies - the style most AP English students learn for research papers, though the synthesis FRQ does not require full MLA formatting. AP English Language Unit 8 references MLA primarily for context: the synthesis FRQ uses in-text attribution by source letter or author name rather than full MLA in-text citations with page numbers, but students should know MLA conventions for other writing. The 2021 MLA Handbook (9th edition) is the current standard. Strong AP students transfer MLA's commitment to attribution and source integrity even when the FRQ format does not demand the full apparatus.

questions.prompt: List of sources at essay end.
questions.explanation: Works cited is the alphabetical list of sources at the end of an MLA-formatted essay - a feature not required by the AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) but central to research writing. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches Works Cited primarily for transfer to research writing in other courses: the FRQ format substitutes the provided source packet for the Works Cited list. Strong students recognize that the source packet performs the same function: making sources locatable and verifiable. The MLA Handbook (9th ed., 2021) sets current Works Cited standards. Students applying MLA outside the FRQ should know container framework, hanging indent, alphabetical order, and full publication information.

In-text citation is brief attribution within the essay's prose - typically (Source A) or 'As Carson notes...' in the synthesis FRQ (Q1) format. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches in-text citation as the integration mechanism: every quotation, paraphrase, and summary needs clear attribution at the point of use, not just in a separate citation list. The AP synthesis FRQ rubric (Row B, evidence and commentary) implicitly demands in-text citation because evidence credit requires the reader to identify which source supplies the cited material. Strong essays cite smoothly within sentence flow: 'Source D's data complicates this claim,' rather than awkward parenthetical interruptions.

Plagiarism in synthesis writing is the use of source material without proper credit - including paraphrases, ideas, and structural borrowings, not just direct quotations. AP English Language Unit 8 teaches plagiarism prevention as foundational to ethical source use: students must attribute everything not their own. The College Board CED 2024 emphasizes academic integrity. On the AP synthesis FRQ (Q1), unattributed source material costs Row B evidence credit and can trigger academic-integrity reviews if egregious. The standard test is whether a reader could mistake source material for the student's original prose; when in doubt, attribute. Over-attribution is always safer than under-attribution.

questions.prompt: First FRQ on AP Lang exam.
questions.explanation: The synthesis essay is the first FRQ on the AP English Language exam - the question requiring students to integrate multiple source perspectives into an original argument. AP English Language Unit 9 frames the synthesis FRQ (Q1) as the most procedurally complex of the three free-response questions because it requires evaluating and integrating an external source packet under timed conditions. The AP English Language exam (College Board CED 2024) allocates approximately 40 minutes for the synthesis essay within the 2-hour-15-minute free-response section, plus 15 minutes of dedicated reading time at the section's start. The 6-point rubric (Row A thesis 1, Row B evidence/commentary 4, Row C sophistication 1) governs scoring.

At least three is the minimum number of sources students must incorporate from the synthesis FRQ (Q1) packet to earn evidence points. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches three as the procedural floor specified by the College Board CED 2024: 'incorporate at least three of the provided sources to support an argument that responds to the prompt.' Students who cite only two sources, no matter how skillfully, cap their Row B evidence credit. Strong essays typically engage four or five sources, though depth of integration matters more than count. The synthesis FRQ rubric explicitly rewards judicious source selection and use.

Roughly 40 minutes is the recommended time allocation for the synthesis FRQ (Q1) on the AP English Language exam, within the 2-hour-15-minute free-response section. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches time management as a foundational FRQ skill: students who spend too long on synthesis lose time for FRQ 2 (rhetorical analysis) and FRQ 3 (argument), and weak time management has compounding effects across the three essays. The 15-minute dedicated reading period at the start of the free-response section is designed to give students time to read the synthesis source packet before the writing clock starts. Strong test-takers use the reading period to mark up sources, identify key evidence, and draft a working thesis.

Original argument is the central goal of the synthesis FRQ (Q1): a thesis that is the student's own, not reducible to any single source's position - the synthesis essay's defining quality. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches that original argument distinguishes synthesis from research compilation: students must take a stance the source packet supports but does not state. The AP synthesis FRQ rubric (Row A, 1 point, College Board CED 2024) requires a defensible thesis; Row C (1 point) rewards sophistication through 'engaging effectively with broader context, demonstrating audience awareness, and offering a nuanced argument.' Pure summary of sources earns zero on both Row A and Row C.

questions.prompt: Number of sources in source packet.
questions.explanation: Seven sources is the typical count in the AP synthesis FRQ (Q1) source packet, though the College Board CED 2024 specifies that the packet contains six or seven sources. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches that students should engage four or five of the packet's sources for strong essays, not all seven and not just three: the rubric rewards depth over count. The source packet always includes both textual and visual sources (the visual is sometimes a chart, sometimes a photograph, sometimes a cartoon), and at least one primary source. Skilled test-takers learn to identify which sources offer the strongest evidence for their developing thesis and focus depth on those.

questions.prompt: Second FRQ on AP Lang exam.
questions.explanation: Rhetorical analysis is the second FRQ on the AP English Language exam - the question requiring students to analyze how an author's rhetorical choices contribute to purpose. AP English Language Unit 9 frames the rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) as the question most directly testing the course's central skills: identifying rhetorical strategies, analyzing their effects, and connecting them to author purpose with a defensible thesis. The College Board CED 2024 specifies the format: students receive one passage (typically 600-800 words) and analyze how the author's rhetorical choices achieve purpose. The 6-point rubric (Row A thesis 1, Row B evidence/commentary 4, Row C sophistication 1) applies as on other FRQs.

How the author achieves purpose is the central focus of the rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) - not what the author says, but how the author's rhetorical choices contribute to making the case. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches that the question stem on the rhetorical analysis FRQ always uses 'how' language because the analytical task is to trace the operation of rhetorical choices, not to summarize the passage's content. The College Board CED 2024 specifies: 'analyze how the writer's choices contribute to the intended meaning and/or purpose of the text.' Strong essays identify specific choices (diction, syntax, organization, tone, appeals, devices) and connect each to specific effects.

Analyzing rhetorical choices is the central skill tested by the rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) - the ability to identify what a writer is doing rhetorically and explain why it matters. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches that rhetorical analysis means going beyond device identification to claims about effect: 'the author uses anaphora' is identification; 'the anaphora of "we cannot" intensifies the impossibility the speaker confronts' is analysis. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ rubric (Row B, up to 4 points evidence and commentary) explicitly rewards analytical commentary over mere identification. The College Board CED 2024 makes 'analysis of rhetorical choices' the central learning outcome of the course.

Single text is the source structure of the rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2): students analyze one passage (typically 600-800 words) rather than the multi-source packet of FRQ 1. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches that the single-text focus demands depth of engagement: students cannot retreat into surface citation as they sometimes do with multi-source synthesis. The rhetorical analysis FRQ rubric (Row B, evidence and commentary) rewards close reading - tracing specific textual evidence (quoted phrases, identified passages) to specific rhetorical effects. The passage is usually nonfiction prose from speeches, essays, letters, or memoir; canonical authors include MLK, Lincoln, Carson, Orwell, and Wollstonecraft.

Rhetorical strategies are the choices students must identify in the rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) essay - the textual features through which the author achieves purpose. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches that rhetorical strategies include diction, syntax, organization, tone, the three appeals (logos/pathos/ethos), kairos, and specific devices (anaphora, metaphor, allusion, parallelism, etc.). The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ rubric (Row B, up to 4 points) rewards specific strategy identification tied to specific effects: vague claims about 'effective writing' or 'powerful language' lose points because they do not analyze what is happening rhetorically. The College Board CED 2024 emphasizes naming strategies precisely.

questions.prompt: Third FRQ on AP Lang exam.
questions.explanation: The argument essay is the third FRQ on the AP English Language exam - the question requiring students to develop their own position on a question posed in the prompt, supported by reasoning and evidence from their own knowledge. AP English Language Unit 9 frames the argument FRQ (Q3) as the question most like the writing students will do in college: defending a defensible position with appropriate evidence and engaging counterargument. The College Board CED 2024 specifies the format: prompt presents a question or quotation, student takes a defensible position responsive to it. The 6-point rubric (Row A thesis 1, Row B evidence/commentary 4, Row C sophistication 1) governs scoring.

Developing position is the central task of the argument FRQ (Q3) - taking a clear stance on the prompt's question and building a case for it through reasoning and evidence. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches that the argument FRQ rewards defensible positions, not safe equivocations: 'this is complex' or 'both sides have merits' positions earn zero on Row A because they fail to take a defensible stance. The AP argument FRQ rubric (Row A, 1 point, College Board CED 2024) requires a thesis that 'takes a position on the issue/topic provided in the prompt.' Strong AP arguments take specific positions while acknowledging complexity through qualifier and counterargument.

From experience or reading is the College Board's specified evidence source for the argument FRQ (Q3): students draw evidence from their own knowledge - personal experience, historical knowledge, current events, literary reading, course content. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches that the argument FRQ rewards diverse evidence types: history, literature, current events, scientific findings, and personal experience all count when relevant. The College Board CED 2024 directs students to 'support [the position] with appropriate evidence' from their own background. The AP argument FRQ rubric (Row B, up to 4 points) does not specify required evidence types but rewards relevance and specificity over breadth.

A quote-based prompt is one common format of the argument FRQ (Q3): a passage from a published author is presented, and students respond by taking a position on the claim or question the passage raises. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches that quote-based prompts require students to engage the quoted author's argument before staking their own position - paraphrasing, agreeing with, qualifying, or refuting. The College Board has used quotes from authors including Mary Shelley, Robert Reich, and Susan Sontag in recent argument FRQs. Strong essays make the quoted author's position clear before introducing their own, demonstrating careful reading.

Convince reader is the rhetorical goal of the argument FRQ (Q3) - the student must persuade an academic reader to accept her position through reasoning and evidence. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches that persuasion combines all three Aristotelian appeals: logos through reasoning chains and evidence, ethos through fair-minded persona and earned authority, pathos through resonant examples. The AP argument FRQ rubric (Row C, sophistication, 1 point, College Board CED 2024) rewards essays that 'demonstrate a nuanced understanding... by employing a style that is consistently vivid and persuasive' - language that explicitly rewards persuasive rhetoric. Strong essays earn the reader's agreement through demonstrated quality.

Six points is the total possible score on each FRQ - the AP English Language exam scoring scale established in 2019 (replacing the older 9-point rubric). AP English Language Unit 9 frames the 6-point rubric as standardizing scoring across the three FRQs: Row A (thesis) earns 0 or 1 point, Row B (evidence and commentary) earns 0-4 points, Row C (sophistication) earns 0 or 1 point. Total: 0-6 points per essay, 0-18 across the three FRQs (College Board CED 2024). Students must learn the rubric structure cold because earning each row's points requires meeting different specific criteria. The 6-point rubric replaced the 9-point holistic scale to make scoring more transparent.

One point is awarded for Row A (thesis) on each AP English Language FRQ - 0 if the thesis is missing, restates the prompt, summarizes sources without taking a position, or makes only an obvious/general claim; 1 if the thesis 'takes a defensible position' responsive to the prompt. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches Row A as the most reliable single point: students who take a clear, defensible position responsive to the prompt earn it regardless of evidence quality. The College Board CED 2024 emphasizes that the thesis can appear in the introduction or conclusion. Weak thesis types lose this point: pure restatement, both-sides equivocation, vague claims, unsupportable absolutes.

Four points is the maximum awarded for Row B (evidence and commentary) on each AP English Language FRQ - the largest single scoring category, distinguishing weak from strong essays. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches Row B as the analytical heart of the rubric: 0 points for minimal or no evidence; 1 point for general evidence without commentary; 2 points for specific evidence with mostly description; 3 points for specific evidence with commentary connecting to thesis; 4 points for specific evidence with consistent and thorough commentary explaining how the evidence supports the line of reasoning. The College Board CED 2024 makes Row B the focus of essay quality.

One point is awarded for Row C (sophistication) on each AP English Language FRQ - the rubric's reward for demonstrating complex argument, vivid persuasive style, or engagement with broader context. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches sophistication as 'the most-missed point' because it requires going beyond competent execution: the College Board CED 2024 awards Row C only when the response 'demonstrates a nuanced understanding,' 'articulates implications or limitations,' 'situates the argument in a broader context,' or 'employs a style that is consistently vivid and persuasive.' Strong essays earn sophistication through complex argument, qualified claims, vivid prose, or contextual awareness.

Sophistication is the most-missed rubric category on the AP English Language FRQs - the Row C point earned by demonstrating complex argument, vivid persuasive style, or engagement with broader context. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches sophistication as the elusive marker distinguishing 5-point essays from 6-point essays. The College Board CED 2024 awards Row C only when the essay 'crafts a nuanced argument by consistently identifying and exploring complexities or tensions,' 'articulates the implications or limitations of an argument by situating it in a broader context,' or 'employs a style that is consistently vivid and persuasive.' Students earn sophistication through qualified claims, counterargument engagement, contextual awareness, or stylistic distinction.

Two hours and fifteen minutes is the total time allocated for the free-response section of the AP English Language exam - the time students have for all three FRQs plus a dedicated reading period. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches time management as a foundational FRQ skill: the College Board CED 2024 structures the free-response section as 15 minutes of dedicated reading time followed by 2 hours for writing the three essays. Students who manage time poorly lose points by leaving FRQ 3 (typically the argument essay) unfinished. Strong test-takers allocate roughly 40 minutes per essay and respect the time limit even when an essay feels incomplete.

Fifteen minutes is the dedicated reading period at the start of the AP English Language free-response section - time students can use to read all three prompts, examine the synthesis FRQ source packet, and begin planning. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches the reading period as critical for the synthesis FRQ (Q1) because students need time to read and annotate the source packet before writing. The College Board CED 2024 specifies that students cannot begin writing during the reading period but can take notes. Strong test-takers use the 15 minutes primarily for synthesis source mark-up, with quick scans of the other two prompts to begin mental planning.

Forty minutes per FRQ is the recommended time allocation across the three AP English Language essays - typically equal division of the 2-hour writing time after the 15-minute reading period. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches 40 minutes as the planning target, though strong students may spend slightly more time on synthesis (which requires source integration) and less on argument (which draws on prepared knowledge). The College Board CED 2024 does not prescribe per-essay time limits, but the rubric expects essays substantial enough to demonstrate full skill: typically 4-7 paragraphs, 400-800 words. Time spent under 30 minutes typically produces undeveloped essays.

Three is the total number of free-response essays on the AP English Language exam - synthesis (FRQ 1), rhetorical analysis (FRQ 2), and argument (FRQ 3). AP English Language Unit 9 frames the three-essay structure as the College Board's standardization of essential AP Lang skills: synthesis tests source integration, rhetorical analysis tests close reading of prose, argument tests independent position-taking. The College Board CED 2024 confirms this structure has been stable since the 2019 redesign. The three FRQs together constitute 55% of the exam score (Multiple Choice = 45%), and the three essays carry equal weight in the FRQ score.

One hour is the time allocated for the multiple-choice section of the AP English Language exam - 60 minutes for 45 MC questions covering reading and writing skills. AP English Language Unit 9 teaches MC structure: the College Board CED 2024 specifies 23-25 reading questions (analyzing nonfiction passages) and 20-22 writing questions (revising and improving a draft). The MC section is 45% of the total exam score; the FRQ section is 55%. Strong students manage MC time as approximately 80 seconds per question, leaving 5-10 minutes to review flagged items. The MC section ends before the FRQ section begins; the two sections are not interruptible.

Logos, the Aristotelian appeal to logic from 'Rhetoric' (4th c. BCE), persuades through evidence, reasoning, and clear inference. AP English Language treats logos as one of three artistic proofs alongside ethos and pathos, with kairos added in modern rhetoric. Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) builds logos through chemical evidence; Sandel's 'Justice' (2009) builds logos through philosophical reasoning.
