The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2, 6-point rubric, College Board CED 2024) awards Row B evidence/commentary points for logos analysis tied to specific reasoning structures - syllogisms, inductive chains, causal arguments - rather than generic 'use of facts' labels. Logos rarely persuades alone; Aristotle insisted the three appeals work together.

Pathos, the Aristotelian appeal to emotion from 'Rhetoric' (Book II, 4th c. BCE), moves audiences through imagery, anecdote, charged diction, and identification with shared values. AP English Language teaches pathos as essential but ethically loaded: pathos aligned with logos and ethos persuades legitimately; pure pathos manipulates. King's 'I Have a Dream' (1963) deploys pathos through 'little black boys and black girls joining hands'; Sojourner Truth's 1851 speech mobilizes pathos through her counted lost children. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric awards Row B points for pathos analysis tied to specific textual moves rather than generic labels.

Ethos, the Aristotelian appeal to credibility from 'Rhetoric' (Book I, 4th c. BCE), persuades through the speaker's expertise, fairness, good character, and good will toward audience. AP English Language teaches that ethos has three components per Aristotle: phronesis (practical wisdom), arete (virtue), and eunoia (good will). King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) builds ethos by citing Aquinas, Augustine, Buber, Tillich, and Niebuhr; Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) leveraged her credentials as marine biologist. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric awards Row B points for ethos analysis tied to specific textual moves: concession, qualification, citation, demonstrated knowledge.

Kairos is the classical Greek rhetorical concept of the right or opportune moment - timeliness as a fourth dimension beyond Aristotle's three artistic proofs. AP English Language teaches kairos as essential to rhetorical success: the same argument deployed at the wrong moment fails. JFK's Inaugural (January 1961) seized kairos at the Cold War threshold; King's 'I Have a Dream' (August 1963) chose the Lincoln Memorial centenary of the Emancipation Proclamation; Lincoln's Second Inaugural (March 1865) caught the kairos of war's final weeks for reconciliation. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) increasingly awards Row C sophistication points for kairotic analysis grounding rhetorical choices in their historical moment.

Aristotle (384-322 BCE), Greek philosopher and student of Plato, systematized the three artistic proofs - logos, ethos, pathos - in his treatise 'Rhetoric' (4th c. BCE), the foundational text of AP English Language. Aristotle defined rhetoric as 'the faculty of observing in any given case the available means of persuasion,' treating it as a counterpart to dialectic. His framework was extended by Roman rhetoricians Cicero ('De Oratore,' 55 BCE) and Quintilian ('Institutio Oratoria,' c. 95 CE), whose five canons (invention, arrangement, style, memory, delivery) the AP CED still references. Students must cite Aristotle by name when identifying rhetorical appeals on the AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2).

Metaphor is a comparison without 'like' or 'as' - an implicit identification between two unlike things. AP English Language teaches metaphor as the most powerful trope (Aristotle, 'Poetics,' 4th c. BCE: 'the greatest thing by far is to be a master of metaphor'). King's 'I Have a Dream' (1963) builds extended metaphor of America's 'promissory note' to Black citizens; Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) builds metaphor of the silenced soundscape. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2, 6-point rubric, College Board CED 2024) rubric rewards specific metaphor analysis tied to the source/target mapping and its rhetorical effect, not generic 'figurative language' labels. Strong metaphor analysis traces what is mapped onto what and why.

Simile is a comparison using 'like' or 'as' - the explicit counterpart to metaphor, marking the comparison as analogy rather than asserting identity. AP English Language distinguishes simile from metaphor: 'America is a melting pot' (metaphor) vs 'America is like a melting pot' (simile). Memoir and personal essay writers often prefer similes for their explicit signaling: Amy Tan's 'Mother Tongue' (1990) deploys similes in describing her mother's English. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rewards students who analyze similes specifically rather than treating them as weaker metaphors - simile's explicitness can be its rhetorical strength, signaling careful comparison rather than asserted identity.

Anaphora is the repetition of the same word or phrase at the beginning of successive clauses, sentences, or paragraphs - one of the most powerful schemes in oral rhetoric. AP English Language treats anaphora as central because it appears constantly in canonical texts: King's 'I Have a Dream' (1963) builds 'I have a dream' and 'Let freedom ring' as anaphoric pillars; Lincoln's Gettysburg Address (1863) closes with 'of the people, by the people, for the people.' The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards anaphora analysis tied to emphasis, rhythm, and structural framing rather than mere identification. Anaphora works through cumulative repetition that builds rhetorical momentum.

Hyperbole is deliberate exaggeration for rhetorical effect - overstating beyond literal truth to convey intensity, urgency, or emotion. AP English Language teaches hyperbole as a trope readers must recognize as intentional rather than literal: the writer is not lying but signaling emotional truth through measured overstatement. Frederick Douglass's 1852 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' deploys hyperbolic language about American hypocrisy that exceeds literal claim while accurately naming moral intensity. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who identify hyperbole and analyze its function - typically intensifying pathos while maintaining ethos through audience recognition of intentional exaggeration.

Paradox is an apparent contradiction that reveals deeper truth - a statement that seems self-defeating but illuminates rather than collapses on examination. AP English Language teaches paradox as related to but larger than oxymoron: paradoxes are full statements while oxymorons are compressed phrases. Orwell's 'Politics and the English Language' (1946) traffics in paradox about how clear thought requires clear language and clear language requires clear thought. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who identify paradox and trace how it functions - typically forcing readers through apparent contradiction toward more sophisticated understanding, building ethos through demonstrated intellectual depth.

A thesis is the main claim of an essay - the controlling argument the rest of the text exists to defend. AP English Language frames the thesis as the make-or-break feature of any argument essay: without a defensible thesis, no amount of evidence or style can earn full credit. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row A, 1 point, College Board CED 2024) requires a thesis that takes a defensible position responsive to the prompt; pure restatement, vague claims, or absolutist positions earn zero. Strong AP theses are specific, defensible, responsive, and complex enough to organize the body paragraphs that follow. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) sets out its thesis early.

A warrant is the reasoning principle connecting evidence to claim - the often-unstated assumption that licenses the inference, from Stephen Toulmin's 'The Uses of Argument' (1958). AP English Language makes warrant analysis central because most argument failures occur not at evidence but at warrant: writers assume connecting principles audiences do not share. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row B, evidence and commentary, up to 4 points) explicitly rewards commentary that surfaces and defends warrants - this is what distinguishes commentary from mere repetition. Strong AP essays make warrants explicit when audiences might resist them. Toulmin's six-part model (claim, data, warrant, backing, qualifier, rebuttal) structures AP rhetoric.

Counterargument is the opposing view acknowledged within one's own argument - either accepted as a limit or refuted in turn. AP English Language treats counterargument as essential to mature argument because ignoring opposition signals either ignorance or evasion, both fatal to ethos. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row C, sophistication, 1 point, College Board CED 2024) explicitly rewards 'engaging effectively with broader context' and 'addressing complexities or tensions' - language that demands counterargument engagement. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) systematically engages each clergy charge before refuting; essays that fail to engage obvious objections cap at 4 of 6 regardless of style.

Refutation is the move of disproving or undermining the counterargument - the active critique completing the rhetorical work of acknowledging opposition. AP English Language teaches refutation as following concession in the Toulmin scheme: the writer concedes what is true in the opposing view, then identifies its decisive failure. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) refutes the charge of extremism by reframing himself in the company of Jesus, Paul, Luther, Lincoln, and Jefferson - inverting the term's valence. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row B, evidence and commentary) rewards refutation engaging the strongest version of opposition (steel-man) rather than caricature (straw man).

Rogerian argument (Carl Rogers, 'On Becoming a Person,' 1961; codified for composition by Young, Becker, and Pike, 'Rhetoric: Discovery and Change,' 1970) is the approach seeking common ground rather than victory - establishing shared interests and acknowledging the opposing view's strengths before introducing one's position. AP English Language contrasts Rogerian argument with Aristotelian (adversarial) and Toulminian (analytical) approaches: Rogerian is well-suited to deeply polarized debates where confrontation hardens opposition. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) blends Aristotelian and Rogerian features. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row C) rewards Rogerian moves on contentious topics.

Diction is the writer's word choice - the selection of vocabulary that shapes meaning, tone, register, and persona. AP English Language treats diction as one of the two primary stylistic dimensions (alongside syntax) analyzed on the rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2). Skilled rhetorical analysts move past 'the author uses strong word choice' to specific diction-effect claims: which words, which connotations, which register, which emotional charge. Orwell's 'Politics and the English Language' (1946) makes diction the central concern, arguing pretentious diction conceals political reality. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ rubric (Row B) awards points for diction analysis tied to author purpose, not vague labels.

Syntax is the arrangement of words in sentences - sentence structure, word order, length, and complexity - the second primary stylistic dimension alongside diction. AP English Language treats syntax as central to the rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) because syntactic choices shape pacing, emphasis, and reader experience independently of vocabulary. Sophisticated rhetorical analysts move beyond identifying sentence types to claims about syntactic effect: short sentences for punch, long periodic sentences for suspension, parallel structure for emphasis. Orwell's 'Politics and the English Language' (1946) addresses syntax directly, advocating short Anglo-Saxon constructions over Latinate inversions for political honesty.

Tone is the author's attitude toward the subject matter, conveyed through diction, syntax, imagery, and stylistic choices - distinct from mood (the audience's emotional state) and voice (the author's overall stylistic signature). AP English Language makes tone central to rhetorical analysis because every text adopts an attitude that shapes how readers receive its claims. Swift's 'A Modest Proposal' (1729) sustains a deadpan, calmly reasonable tone that intensifies the savage indignation beneath it. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric (Row B) awards points for tonal analysis using precise adjectives (reverent, indignant, ironic, conciliatory, urgent) tied to specific textual evidence rather than vague labels.

Imagery is language that evokes sensory experience - sights, sounds, textures, tastes, smells - making abstract claims viscerally concrete. AP English Language teaches imagery as foundational to pathos: readers feel imagery before they reason about it. King's 'I Have a Dream' (1963) deploys imagery of 'the table of brotherhood' and 'little black boys and black girls joining hands with little white boys and white girls'; Carson's 'Silent Spring' (1962) opens with imagery of a silent suburban morning emptied of birdsong. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards specific imagery identification tied to its effect rather than vague claims that 'the author uses descriptive language.'

Voice is the distinctive style of a writer - the cumulative effect of diction, syntax, tone, rhythm, and habitual rhetorical choices that makes prose recognizable. AP English Language distinguishes voice (the writer's signature across works) from tone (attitude in one passage) and from persona (a constructed speaker different from author). Amy Tan's 'Mother Tongue' (1990) develops a voice marked by code-switching between standard and 'family' English. Orwell's voice is plainspoken yet razor-edged. The AP rhetorical analysis FRQ (Q2) rubric rewards students who identify voice features specifically rather than calling all distinctive prose 'unique' - voice analysis must trace features to recognizable choices.

The synthesis essay is FRQ 1 on the AP English Language exam - the question requiring students to integrate multiple source perspectives into an original argument. AP English Language frames the synthesis FRQ (Q1) as the most procedurally complex of the three free-response questions because it requires evaluating and integrating an external source packet under timed conditions. The AP English Language exam (College Board CED 2024) allocates approximately 40 minutes for the synthesis essay within the 2-hour-15-minute free-response section, plus 15 minutes of dedicated reading time. The 6-point rubric (Row A thesis 1, Row B evidence/commentary 4, Row C sophistication 1) governs scoring.

Rhetorical analysis is FRQ 2 on the AP English Language exam - the question requiring students to analyze how an author's rhetorical choices contribute to purpose. AP English Language frames the rhetorical analysis FRQ as the question most directly testing the course's central skills: identifying rhetorical strategies, analyzing their effects, and connecting them to author purpose with a defensible thesis. The College Board CED 2024 specifies the format: students receive one passage (typically 600-800 words) and analyze how the author's rhetorical choices achieve purpose. Strong responses use specific textual evidence to support claims about effect, going beyond identification to analysis. The 6-point rubric applies as on other FRQs.

The argument essay is FRQ 3 on the AP English Language exam - the question requiring students to develop their own position on a question posed in the prompt, supported by reasoning and evidence from their own knowledge. AP English Language frames the argument FRQ as the question most like the writing students will do in college: defending a defensible position with appropriate evidence and engaging counterargument. The College Board CED 2024 specifies the format: prompt presents a question or quotation, student takes a defensible position. The 6-point rubric (Row A thesis 1, Row B evidence/commentary 4, Row C sophistication 1) governs scoring.

Six points is the total possible score on each FRQ - the AP English Language exam scoring scale established in 2019 (replacing the older 9-point rubric). AP English Language frames the 6-point rubric as standardizing scoring across the three FRQs: Row A (thesis) earns 0 or 1 point, Row B (evidence and commentary) earns 0-4 points, Row C (sophistication) earns 0 or 1 point. Total: 0-6 points per essay, 0-18 across the three FRQs (College Board CED 2024). Students must learn the rubric structure cold because earning each row's points requires meeting different specific criteria. The 6-point rubric replaced the 9-point holistic scale to make scoring more transparent.

Ad hominem (Latin: 'to the person') is the logical fallacy of attacking the speaker rather than the argument - a deflection from substance to character. AP English Language distinguishes ad hominem from legitimate ethos challenges: questioning a witness's credibility when credibility is at issue is not fallacious, but dismissing a chemical claim because the speaker is unpopular is. The AP argument FRQ (Q3) rubric (Row C, sophistication) penalizes students who refute opposing views through ad hominem rather than substantive engagement. King's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' (1963) explicitly refuses ad hominem against the clergymen, engaging their arguments instead - a move that strengthens his own ethos.
